coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of N, S-doped carbon dots with green emission and their application in 4-NP detection, pH sensing, and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, YingTe
Wu, RongRong
Zhang, YingJie
Cheng, SiRong
Wang, BoHan
Zhang, Yong
Zhang, YuanYuan
coredata.dc:description: Using p-aminophenol and dithioacetamide as precursors, green luminescent nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) are prepared by hydrothermal method with the quantum yield (QY) of 7.1 %. Superior properties of the N, S-CDs including high photostability, outstanding biocompatibility, and desirable biological penetration were found, which could realize the monitor of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and pH. The N, S-CDs can be designed as a fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of 4-NP in water samples with linear ranges of 0–85 µM and a detection limit of 0.037 µM. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs is sensitive to pH and shows a linear relationship with pH values ranging from 3.50 to 7.25 and 7.25–12.00, accompanied with a significant color variation of the N, S-CDs solution from colorless to brown. Finally, the proposed N, S-CDs have also been applied to monitor the 4-NP in oocysts due to its low cytotoxicity, providing a great capacity for various targets molecules detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doping
4-nitrophenol (4-NP)
pH sensor
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image, (b) particle size distribution, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) FT-IR spectra of N, S-CDs.
(a) XPS full spectrum (b) C1s (c) N1s (d) S2p spectra of N, S-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line), fluorescence excitation spectrum (blue line) and fluorescence emission spectrum (red line) of N, S-CDs. Inset: photos of N, S-CDs solution under natural light (left) and ultraviolet light (365 nm) (right); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (290–460 nm).
The influences of different (a) organics and (b) cations on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs separately.
(a) The N, S-CDs fluorescence intensity after adding different concentrations of 4-NP (0–85 µM). (b) Relationship between F0-F and the concentrations of 4-NP. (λex = 327 nm, λem = 520 nm).

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N, S-CDs, 4-NP and N, S-CDs + 4-NP; (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP and fluorescence excitation spectrum of N, S-CDs; (c) Fluorescence decays decay of N, S-CDs in the absence and presence of 4-NP.
(a)The fluorescence intensity changes with different pH (3.50–7.25) BR buffers. (b) Standard curve (c) Reversible fluorescence response curve in seven consecutive pH cycles between 3.50 and 7.25.
(a) The fluorescence intensity changes with different pH (7.25–12.00) BR buffers. (b) Standard curve (c) Reversible fluorescence response curve in seven consecutive pH cycles between 7.25 and –12.00.
Confocal fluorescence imaging of oocysts co-incubated with N, S-CDs (before and after adding 4-NP as a contrast).
Schematic diagram of the synthesis and application of the N, S-CDs.
Comparison of as-reported CDs for 4-NP sensing and pH response.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of N, S-doped carbon dots with green emission and their application in 4-NP detection, pH sensing, and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: YangQuan NO. 7 Middle School of ShanXi, YangQuan 045000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: YangQuan NO. 7 Middle School of ShanXi

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: YangQuan NO. 7 Middle School of ShanXi, YangQuan 045000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, McGill University, Montreal, Quebec H3A0E9, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, McGill University, Montreal, Quebec H3A0E9, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we synthesized green luminescent nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) which could realize the monitor of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and pH. The N, S-CDs can be designed as a fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of 4-NP in water samples with linear ranges of 0–85 µM and a detection limit of 0.037 µM. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs is sensitive to pH and shows a linear relationship with pH values ranging from 3.50 to 7.25 and 7.25–12.00, accompanied with a significant color variation of the N, S-CDs solution from colorless to brown. Finally, the proposed N, S-CDs have also been applied to monitor the 4-NP in oocysts due to its low cytotoxicity.
Scheme 1. Schematic diagram of the synthesis and application of the N, S-CDs.

Using p-aminophenol and dithioacetamide as precursors, green luminescent nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) are prepared by hydrothermal method with the quantum yield (QY) of 7.1 %. Superior properties of the N, S-CDs including high photostability, outstanding biocompatibility, and desirable biological penetration were found, which could realize the monitor of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and pH. The N, S-CDs can be designed as a fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of 4-NP in water samples with linear ranges of 0–85 µM and a detection limit of 0.037 µM. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs is sensitive to pH and shows a linear relationship with pH values ranging from 3.50 to 7.25 and 7.25–12.00, accompanied with a significant color variation of the N, S-CDs solution from colorless to brown. Finally, the proposed N, S-CDs have also been applied to monitor the 4-NP in oocysts due to its low cytotoxicity, providing a great capacity for various targets molecules detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aromatic nitro compounds, as a kind of explosives, not only have serious harm to animals, plants and aquatic organisms, but also have serious toxicity to human beings [1]. Especially 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), because of its good water solubility and stability, can exist in water for a long time, even if ingested in a short period of time, it will cause nausea and vomiting [2]. On the other hand, 4-NP is widely used as intermediates in pharmaceutical, dyestuffs, pesticides, insecticides, leather fungicides, acid-base indicators [3] and other chemical processes, which will inevitably be discharged into the environment. Consequently, it is imperative to adopt a special green method to detect 4-NP [4].

It has been reported that pH sensors can be made of semiconductor quantum dots [5,6], nanoparticles [7,8], and fluorescent hydrogel [9–11], etc. However, semiconductor quantum dots and fluorescent hydrogel typically require multi-step and complex preparation processes, which limits their applications [12–14]. Therefore, nanoparticle-based fluorescent pH sensors have attracted much attention [15]. And hence, it is important to develop stable and environmentally friendly fluorescence materials for pH detection.

In recent years, fluorescence sensors with simple prepared procedures, high sensitivity and good specificity have received more and more attention in the fields of biology, physiology and environmental science, especially in the detection of small molecules [16] and metal ions [17–20]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new generation of outstanding fluorescent nanomaterials, have distinct superior properties such as high fluorescence quantum yield (QY), excellent biocompatibility, and high photochemical stability [21,22]. Based on these outstanding characteristics, CDs have attracted the interests of researchers in biological imaging [23], nanomedicine [24], photocatalysis [25], and electrocatalysis [26]. In addition, CDs are widely used in various analytical fields [27,28], due to its wide range of excitation correlation emission spectrum. As a fluorescence sensor, CDs has excellent performance in detecting heavy metal ions [29], small organic molecules [30] and the pH of water in the environment [31]. For instance, Qu et al. [1] synthesized blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with fluorescence response to 4-NP, and the color response of 4-NP from colorless solution to yellow can be observed with the naked eye. Fan et al. [28] prepared pH responsive carbon quantum dots (Si-CDs) with high luminescence through a one-step hydrothermal method, which can control the significant green blue purple transition by changing the pH value. It remains a challenging issue to develop CDs with pH-sensitive and 4-NP response behavior accompanied by the significant visual fluorescence changes.

Here, green N, S-CDs are prepared by the one-step hydrothermal method with 4-aminophenol and dithioacetamide as precursors. The prepared N, S-CDs present favorable water solubility and excitation-related fluorescence properties as expected. Furthermore, it is discovered that the N, S-CDs is sensitive to pH values ranging from 3.50 to 7.25 and 7.25–12.00 accompanied by the visible varying from colorless to brown. More importantly, there is a good linear relationship between the intensity of N, S-CDs and the concentration of 4-NP ranging in 0–85 µM. N, S-CDs have good sensitivity for 4-NP in actual water samples. The pH and 4-NP sensitivity give them great potential as pH sensors and 4-NP detectors. Scheme 1 shows the preparation process and application of the N, S-CDs.
The materials used in this work and the instruments which are used for the characterization of N, S-CDs are listed in the Electronic Supporting Material (ESM).

0.4 g 4-aminophenol and 0.24 g dithioacetamide are dispersed into 50 mL ethanol, the mixture is transferred into a 100 mL high-pressure reaction kettle, and then keep in an oven at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After the above reaction is completed, naturally cool to room temperature to obtain the brown solution. Then the solution is filtered with filter paper to remove larger particles, and dialyzes with a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 24 h. Eventually, the dialysis bag solution is centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min, and the dark brown powder is obtained by the freeze-drying method, which is stored at 4 ℃ for further use. By calculation, the product yield of N, S-CDs is 15.6 %.
The QY is obtained by the method in the literature [32], and details can be found in ESM.

In a typical process, N, S-CDs (v = 20 μL) are added to Britton-Robison (BR) buffer solution (v = 2 mL, pH = 7.25). Subsequently, different concentration of 4-NP is gradually added. When the solution is fully mixed, the corresponding fluorescence intensity is recorded at the excitation wavelength of 327 nm.

To investigate the selectivity of N, S-CDs to 4-NP, 20 μL (C = 0.01 M) of various small molecules (2,4-dinitrophenol, hydroquinone, acetone, picric acid, citric acid, ammonium nitrate, nitrobenzene, urea, 2,4-dichlorophenol, benzoic acid, hydrogen peroxide, ethylenediamine, o-nitrotoluene, o-nitrobenzoic acid, m-hydroxybenzoic acid, m-nitrobenzoic acid, salicylic acid) and various metal ions (Zn2+，Pb2+，Cr3+，Ni2+，Fe3+，Cd2+，Fe2+，Li+，Ag+，Al3+，Co2+，Ca2+，Hg2+，Cu2+，Na+，K+) are added to the N, S-CDs to replace 4-NP. The interference performance is measured when the potential interfering ions coexist with 4-NP. Fluorescence emission spectra are recorded with 327 nm as the fixed excitation wavelength.

In the pH titration experiment, 20 μL of N, S-CDs dispersion is added into 2 mL of BR buffer solution with different pH values. Then the fluorescence emission spectra are recorded with the excitation wavelength of 327 nm. All measurements are performed in triplicate at room temperature.

The practicability of the sensing function of N, S-CDs in real water samples is explored. A real water sample is taken from Lingde Lake of Shanxi University and could be used in the experiment after filtration and centrifugation.

The experimental steps of oocysts bioimaging are as follows: at room temperature, 10 μL of oocysts solution and 10 μL N, S-CDs solution are added to a slide, respectively. Then the oocysts and N, S-CDs solution are co-incubated for 20 min until the N, S-CDs solution do not enter into the oocysts. Afterwards, the oocysts is washed with pure water, and the cell imaging is performed using confocal laser scanning microscope. Lastly, 0.1 M 4-NP solution is added to the oocysts on the slide and incubates for 10 min. The confocal fluorescence imaging is performed again.

To investigate the N, S-CDs nanostructure, TEM is used to directly observe the morphology and particle size distribution. As it can be observed from Fig. 1 a, the synthesized N, S-CDs exhibit a microsphere structure, with an estimated average diameter of about 3.25 nm (Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 1c, the wide diffraction peak at 21.6° from XRD pattern further proves the amorphous structure of the N, S-CDs. As indicated in Fig. 1d, the wide absorption peak at 3239 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H groups; the absorption peak at 1673 cm−1, 1605 cm−1, 1231 cm−1, and 1101 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C = O [33], aromatic C = C, C-O or C-N [34], and C = S. The sharp absorption peaks at 1512 cm−1, 830 cm−1, 619 cm−1 can be ascribed to the bending vibration of N–H; and C–H. The above analysis indicates that N, S-CDs contain aromatic structure and its surface is rich in many oxygen-containing groups, making N, S-CDs highly hydrophilic.

In addition, the structure of the synthesized N, S-CDs was characterized by nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR). 13C NMR spectrum is shown in Figure S1 (ESM). The 13CNMR spectrum reveals C-CH in 0–40 ppm, C-O and C–C = O groups (sp3 carbon atoms) in 40–90 ppm [35]. The aromatic and sp2 carbons are assigned to the signals from 120 to 150 ppm. The structure is further validated by XPS analysis. It is shown that the contents of C, N, O and S in N, S-CDs are 43.09 %, 11.44 %, 31.78 % and 13.7 % (Fig. 2 a), respectively [36]. In Fig. 2b, the high resolution C1s spectrograph reveals the presence of C–C/C = C (284.8 eV), C-S (285.4 eV), C-N (286.5 eV) and C = O (288.7 eV). As shown in Fig. 2c, N1s spectra exhibit two characteristic peaks of C-N and N–H at 399.5 eV and 400.3 eV [37]. Fig. 2d displays two peaks at 163.4 eV and 164.6 eV caused by S2p1/2 and S2p3/2, respectively. The peak at 165.4 eV correspondences to S-C, and the peak at 168.6 eV correspondences to C-S-O [38]. The XPS results are consistent with the FT-IR spectrum analysis, which indicates that N and S are successfully doped into the CDs.

The spectral properties of N, S-CDs including UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectrum and fluorescence QY are investigated. As can be seen from the black line in Fig. 3 a, the weak absorption peak at 221 nm of UV–vis spectrum is ascribed to the π-π* transition of C = C [39]. The maximum fluorescence excitation peak (λex, blue line) and the maximum fluorescence emission peak (λem, red line) are located at 327 nm and 520 nm, respectively. The inset shows that N, S-CDs are pale yellow solution, while under UV light (λ = 365 nm) it displays bright green fluorescence. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs at various excitation wavelengths are investigated. As shown in Fig. 3b, when the excitation wavelengths of N, S-CDs are changed from 290 nm to 460 nm, the optimal emission peak shifts, nonetheless, N, S-CDs demonstrates fluorescence behavior related to the excitation wavelength, which is an inherent property of carbon particles, and this is consistent with previous reported results [40]. Besides, the QY of N, S-CDs is calculated to be 7.1 % by using quinine sulphate as reference.

In order to analyze the selectivity of N, S-CDs, the effects of some small molecules (hydroquinone, acetone, citric acid, urea, benzoic acid, H2O2, 2,4-dinitrophenol, picric acid, ethylenediamine, nitrobenzene, 2,4-dichlorophenol, ammonium nitrate, o-nitrotoluene, m-nitrobenzoic acid, o-nitrobenzoic acid, m-hydroxybenzoic acid, salicylic acid) and metal ions (Zn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Li+, Ag+, Al3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Na+, K+) on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs are investigates, with the concentration of all ions is at 100 µM. In Fig. 4 a and b, only the addition of 4-NP generates apparent fluorescence quenching of N, S-CDs, the changes of fluorescence intensity caused by other small organics and metal ions is negligible. And when small molecules and cations coexist with 4-NP, it does not affect the detection of 4-NP by N, S-CDs (Fig. S2a and b). It demonstrates that N, S-CDs have excellent selectivity and anti-interference for the detection of 4-NP.

Fig. S3 investigated the changes of the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CDs solution after the addition of 4-NP over time. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the system decreases to a minimum within 20 s and remains stable for a longer time thereafter. Consequently, the optimum reaction time of 4-NP with N, S-CDs is 20 s.

The fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs with different concentrations of 4-NP added is shown in Fig. 5 a. With the increasing of 4-NP concentration in the system, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs gradually decreases . A good linear relationship between (F0-F) and 4-NP concentration is observed in the range of 0–85 µM (Fig. 5b), and the linear regression equation is expressed as F0-F = 7.67C-26.24 (R2 = 0.998). In the expression, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs before and after the addition of 4-NP, respectively. C represents the concentration of 4-NP. The detection limit of 4-NP is calculated to be 0.037 µM according to the equation LOD = 3σ/S (σ is the standard deviation of recording background, S is the slope of linear fitting).

Generally speaking, the quenching mechanisms of CDs mainly include static quenching effect (SQE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), internal filtering effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photoinduced Electron transfer (PET). According to the information obtained, DQE, FRET, and PET typically lead to a significant decrease in fluorescence lifetime, while IFE and SQE do not. When the UV visible absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps with the fluorescence spectrum of CDs, IFE usually occurs [41,42]. IFE does not cause changes in the UV–vis. However, significant changes in the UV–vis can be observed when N, S-CDs form ground state complexes with quenchers through SQE [43,44].

As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, the UV visible absorption spectrum of 4-NP overlaps significantly with the fluorescence excitation spectra of N, S-CDs, and the fluorescence lifetime remains unchanged after the addition of 4-NP (Fig. 6c). This fact indicates that the mechanism of quenching N, S-CDs with 4-NP is IFE.

In Fig. S4, the fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs in BR buffer solutions with different pH values are tested. With the increase of pH in the range of 2–7, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs gradually increases. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs gradually decreases with the increasing of pH in the range of 7–12. Under extremely acidic or alkaline conditions, the fluorescence is disappeared which can be attributed to protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs [45]. As shown in the illustration, the visible color of N, S-CDs solution changes from colorless to brown with pH ranging from 2 to 12, and acid and alkali can be distinguished by naked eyes.

As can be seen from Fig. 7 a, with the increasing of pH value from 3.50 to 7.25, the fluorescence emission intensity of N, S-CDs increases. In this range, there is an apparent linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH. In Fig. 7b, the linear regression equation can be defined as F/F0 = 0.2459pH-0.8166 (R2 = 0.996). In addition, to prove that the fluorescence of N, S-CDs are reversible between 3.50 and 7.25, the pH value of N, S-CDs solution is adjusted from 3.50 to 7.25 for seven consecutive cycles. Importantly, the fluorescence responses of N, S-CDs were highly reversible as the pH was cycled between 3.50 and 7.25 as shown in Fig. 7c.

Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs decreases with the increasing of pH from 7.25 to 12.00 (Fig. 8 a). In Fig. 8b, the linear regression equation can be described as F = -138.4pH + 1862.3 (R2 = 0.998). The N, S-CDs solution in alkaline conditions has the carboxyl deprotonated, resulting in low fluorescence intensity [26]. Furthermore, to prove that the fluorescence of N, S-CDs are reversible between 7.25 and 12.00, the pH value of N, S-CDs solution is adjusted from 7.25 to 12.00 in seven consecutive cycles. As shown in Fig. 8c, after seven consecutive pH cycles, there is almost no obvious fluorescence degradation of N, S-CDs. Based on the above analysis results, the N, S-CDs are expected to be developed as a pH sensor.

Compared with other CDs-based 4-NP sensors, N. S-CDs have a lower detection limit (LOD). In addition, the N, S-CDs exhibited a unique pH-sensitive luminescence trait within pH range of 3.50–12.0 and have great linear relationship (Table 1 ).

The 4-NP of tap water and river water samples is tested to assess the reliability of the designed detection method. As shown in Table S1, we can see that the recovery of 4-NP in the spiked samples ranged from 97.9 % to 105.6 %, and the RSD (n = 5) of each test concentration is lower than 2.31 %. These results indicate that this method can be used to measure 4-NP in practical application.

Good biocompatibility makes the prepared N, S-CDs a good candidate for biological imaging. When N, S-CDs are incubated with living cells of oocysts at room temperature for 20 min, as shown in Fig. 9 , we can clearly see that N, S-CDs are well dispersed in oocysts, and the morphology of oocysts remains good with bright green fluorescence emission (λex = 327 nm). After treatment with N, S-CDs, there were no substantial morphological changes in the cells of oocysts, revealing low cytotoxicity of the synthesized N, S-CDs. In addition, after adding 4-NP and irradiating with 327 nm excitation light, it was found that the morphology of oocysts remained good, but the green fluorescence emission within the cells was almost reduced to invisible, indicating that the quenching of N, S-CDs fluorescence by 4-NP can be carried out in the body of oocysts. The above analysis indicates that the N, S-CDs have low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, and can be used for in vivo imaging of oocysts.

In summary, green emission N, S-CDs were conveniently fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal process of 4-aminophenol and dithioacetamide. The N, S-CDs shows good selectivity and sensitivity for 4-NP at the concentration range from 0 to 85 µM, with a detection limit of 0.037 µM. Based on the sensitive response of the N, S-CDs to 4-NP, a sensitive and low-cost sensor for detection of 4-NP was creatively developed, showing a fast detection speed and low limit of detection. And, the sensor has been successfully applied to the determination of 4-NP in actual water samples. More importantly, in the pH range of 3.50–7.25 and 7.25–12.00 respectively, there is a favorable linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH value. In addition, it has been successfully applied to the biological imaging of oocysts in vivo, which widens the horizons for the functional applications of N, S-CDs.

YingTe Wang: Investigation. RongRong Wu: Data curation, Methodology, Visualization. YingJie Zhang: Investigation. SiRong Cheng: Investigation. BoHan Wang: Investigation. Yong Zhang: Project administration. YuanYuan Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Health Commission (Grant No.2020TD14) and the foundation of Shanxi University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123709.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
